evidence: The paper reports "the p-value of the impurity variable importance of a permutation test with 15,000 permutations" (Section 2.4-2.5) but does not interpret magnitude or ranking of importance scores. Figure 3 plots "significance of p-values of each predictor," not importance rankings. The key finding on basophils—"increased basophil counts were associated with increased odds of responding within three months" (p=0.0027, OR 1.3258)—derives from univariate logistic regression effect sizes, not from importance-based rankings. Random forest impurity importance results are supplementary and "not significant after multiple testing correction" (Table 2). Corroboration comes from logistic regression, Wilcoxon test (p=0.0014), and cut-off analysis, all measuring pairwise associations rather than variable selection by importance rank.
